does: This paper demonstrates a novel jailbreaking vulnerability in Large Vision-Language Models where combinations of individually safe images and prompts can collectively produce unsafe outputs. The authors identify two underlying mechanisms enabling this: the universal reasoning capabilities of LVLMs that allow cross-domain inference, and a 'safety snowball effect' where benign inputs accumulate to circumvent safety guardrails. The core contribution is the Safety Snowball Agent (SSA), an agent-based framework that autonomously generates or retrieves jailbreak images through a two-stage process involving initial response generation and iterative refinement, demonstrating that aggregate safety does not guarantee compositional safety in multimodal systems.
connects: This work extends the growing literature on adversarial attacks against vision-language models, building on prior research showing that carefully crafted visual inputs can elicit harmful outputs. It responds to limitations in existing jailbreak studies that assume individual malicious images are necessary, instead drawing insights from research on emergent capabilities and compositional reasoning in large models. The paper connects to work on alignment vulnerabilities and safety testing in LLMs, adapting those insights to the multimodal setting where visual and textual modalities interact in ways not fully captured by single-modality defenses.
enables: This work opens new research directions for understanding and defending against compositional vulnerabilities in multimodal systems. It enables development of more robust safety evaluations that test not just individual inputs but combinations thereof, and motivates the need for safety mechanisms that account for emergent behaviors at the intersection of modalities. The findings suggest future work on detecting and mitigating safety snowball effects through improved training procedures, better architectural safeguards for multimodal reasoning, and evaluation frameworks that systematically probe compositional safety properties in increasingly complex vision-language applications.
